legal.summary: Section 110 empowers the Commissioner to issue enforcement notices requiring a data user to take specified steps to comply with the PDPA within a prescribed period. Non-compliance with an enforcement notice is a criminal offence. Data users have the right to appeal enforcement notices to the Appeal Tribunal under Part VII.

requirements.requirement: Upon receipt of a Commissioner enforcement notice, immediately engage Legal counsel, acknowledge receipt, and prepare a detailed compliance plan addressing each requirement specified in the notice within the prescribed timeframe
requirements.rationale: Non-compliance with an enforcement notice is a separate criminal offence from the underlying violation; time is critical — the organisation must demonstrate it is taking remedial action from the moment the notice is received

requirements.requirement: Where the organisation intends to appeal an enforcement notice to the Appeal Tribunal, file the appeal within the prescribed appeal period while simultaneously progressing compliance with the notice to the extent practicable, and obtain Legal counsel's advice on the grounds of appeal
requirements.rationale: Filing an appeal does not automatically suspend the enforcement notice unless the Tribunal grants a stay; the organisation must pursue both compliance and appeal simultaneously to avoid the additional offence of non-compliance

technical.summary: The organisation must be able to rapidly assess and remediate technical non-compliance identified in enforcement notices, including implementing new controls, modifying systems, or altering processing activities.

requirements.requirement: Establish a rapid remediation protocol that can be activated upon receipt of an enforcement notice, assigning technical resources and a project timeline to implement required changes within the enforcement notice deadline
requirements.rationale: Enforcement notice deadlines may be short; having a pre-established rapid response protocol avoids the organisation being caught without a plan when a notice arrives
requirements.frequency: Protocol established in advance; activated as required

governance.summary: Enforcement notice response must be managed at the most senior level of the organisation, with Board awareness and adequate resourcing for rapid compliance.

requirements.requirement: Escalate receipt of any enforcement notice to the Board or equivalent governing body within 24 hours, and provide regular updates on compliance progress; treat enforcement notice remediation as a priority project with dedicated resourcing
requirements.rationale: Enforcement notices represent the highest level of regulatory escalation short of prosecution; Board-level awareness ensures adequate resources are allocated for rapid compliance

s129A.title: Cross-Border Transfer of Personal Data (2024 Amendment)

legal.summary: Section 129A, inserted by the Personal Data Protection (Amendment) Act 2024 (Act A1699), governs the transfer of personal data outside Malaysia. Data users may only transfer personal data to places outside Malaysia that are specified by the Minister, or where the transfer meets one of the prescribed conditions including data subject consent, adequacy, binding corporate rules, standard contractual clauses, or other approved mechanisms.

requirements.requirement: Before transferring personal data outside Malaysia, verify that the destination country or territory is listed in the Personal Data Protection (Transfer of Personal Data to Places Outside Malaysia) Order, or that one of the alternative transfer mechanisms under s129A applies
requirements.rationale: Section 129A prohibits cross-border transfers except as permitted; an unlawful transfer is an offence; the 2024 Amendment replaced the original s129 with more structured transfer mechanisms aligned with international standards
requirements.frequency: Before each new cross-border transfer arrangement

requirements.requirement: Where relying on standard contractual clauses (SCCs) or binding corporate rules (BCRs) as the transfer mechanism, execute the approved clauses with all overseas recipients before the transfer commences, and retain copies of executed agreements
requirements.rationale: SCCs and BCRs create legally binding obligations on overseas recipients equivalent to Malaysian PDPA standards; execution before transfer is a prerequisite, not a retrospective formality
requirements.frequency: Before each transfer to a new overseas recipient relying on SCCs/BCRs

requirements.requirement: Maintain a cross-border transfer register documenting all transfers of personal data outside Malaysia, the destination country, the transfer mechanism relied on, the categories of data transferred, and the third-party recipient details
requirements.rationale: The transfer register enables the data user to demonstrate compliance with s129A and provides JPDP with the documentation required during inspections; it is also required for data subject access and transparency purposes

requirements.requirement: Where relying on data subject consent as the transfer mechanism, ensure consent is specific to the cross-border transfer (not bundled with other consents), identifies the destination country, and informs the data subject of the risks arising from the absence of a protection standard equivalent to the PDPA
requirements.rationale: Consent for cross-border transfer must be informed and specific; generic consent to data sharing does not extend to cross-border transfers to unprotected jurisdictions

technical.summary: Technical controls must prevent unauthorised cross-border transfers and enforce the approved transfer mechanisms. Data flows must be mapped to identify all cross-border transfers, including those that occur through cloud services and third-party processors.

requirements.requirement: Conduct a comprehensive data flow mapping exercise to identify all cross-border transfers of personal data, including transfers through cloud service providers, SaaS platforms, and third-party integrations, and classify each transfer against the applicable s129A mechanism
requirements.rationale: Many cross-border transfers occur invisibly through cloud services; data flow mapping identifies all transfers (including non-obvious ones) and enables the organisation to ensure each has a lawful basis
requirements.frequency: Annual exercise; updated on new system deployments

requirements.requirement: Implement network and data governance controls to prevent transfers of personal data to non-approved overseas locations, including DLP policies, API gateway controls, and cloud access security broker (CASB) configurations where applicable
requirements.rationale: Technical controls to prevent unauthorised transfers provide a last line of defence when process controls fail; they are particularly important for preventing employee-initiated transfers to non-approved cloud storage

requirements.requirement: Map all cross-border personal data flows in ROPA including transfer mechanisms used; review regularly for new or changed transfers
requirements.rationale: CBPDT Guideline 2025, cbpdt-1 — scope and mapping under s129A

requirements.requirement: Verify receiving country is on Commissioner's adequacy list before each transfer to new destination (UK, EU/EEA, Japan, Korea, NZ, Canada)
requirements.rationale: CBPDT Guideline 2025, cbpdt-2 — adequacy determinations under s129A

requirements.requirement: Execute Commissioner-approved Standard Contractual Clauses before transfer commences; conduct Transfer Impact Assessment; suspend if local law prevents compliance
requirements.rationale: CBPDT Guideline 2025, cbpdt-3 — SCCs under s129A

requirements.requirement: For intra-group transfers: submit BCR application to Commissioner; ensure board endorsement by all entities; renew every 3 years; submit annual compliance reports
requirements.rationale: CBPDT Guideline 2025, cbpdt-4 — BCRs under s129A

requirements.requirement: Verify recipient holds valid certification (ISO 27701, APEC CBPR, GCBPR); monitor for lapsing; plan data repatriation if certification lost
requirements.rationale: CBPDT Guideline 2025, cbpdt-5 — certification schemes under s129A

requirements.requirement: Conduct Transfer Impact Assessment evaluating receiving country legal framework, state access powers, and practical supplementary measures before relying on SCCs or BCRs
requirements.rationale: CBPDT Guideline 2025, cbpdt-7 — TIA under s129A
requirements.frequency: Before transfer; update on material legal changes

requirements.requirement: Execute written DPA with overseas processors before processing begins; require processing only on instructions, equivalent security, breach notification, and end-of-engagement deletion
requirements.rationale: CBPDT Guideline 2025, cbpdt-8 — overseas processor requirements under s129A

governance.summary: Cross-border transfer governance must ensure that all new arrangements involving overseas transfer of personal data are reviewed and approved before implementation, and that existing arrangements are periodically reviewed for continued adequacy.

requirements.requirement: Require DPO and Legal sign-off for all new arrangements involving cross-border transfer of personal data before the arrangement is implemented, including SaaS platform adoptions and international group data sharing agreements
requirements.rationale: Many organisations inadvertently transfer personal data overseas through cloud platform adoptions without realising the cross-border transfer implications; mandatory review prevents these inadvertent violations

requirements.requirement: Conduct annual review of all cross-border transfer arrangements to verify that the transfer mechanism remains appropriate (e.g., that any reliance on adequacy decisions or SCCs remains current), and take corrective action where the basis for a transfer has lapsed or changed
requirements.rationale: Transfer mechanisms can become inadequate over time (e.g., if adequacy status changes or SCCs are superseded); annual review ensures continued lawfulness of ongoing transfers

legal.summary: Section 129B (inserted by A1699) requires prescribed data users to appoint a qualified DPO. The DPO must be registered with the Commissioner within 30 days. Failure to appoint is an offence.

requirements.requirement: Assess whether DPO appointment is mandatory (large-scale monitoring, sensitive data, 200K+ subjects, vulnerable groups, Commissioner direction); document self-assessment
requirements.rationale: DPO Guideline 2025, dpo-1 — mandatory appointment criteria under s129B

requirements.requirement: Appoint DPO with expert PDPA knowledge and relevant certifications (CIPM/CIPP recommended); document qualifications; ensure ongoing professional development
requirements.rationale: DPO Guideline 2025, dpo-2 — qualifications under s129B

technical.summary: Systems must support DPO functions including ROPA maintenance, DPIA management, breach notification workflows, and Commissioner communication. Registration via prescribed portal.

requirements.requirement: Register DPO with Commissioner within 30 days of appointment using prescribed form; notify Commissioner within 30 days of any DPO change; fill vacancy within 60 days
requirements.rationale: DPO Guideline 2025, dpo-6 — registration requirements under s129B

requirements.requirement: Conduct DPIA before high-risk processing (profiling, large-scale sensitive data, systematic monitoring, vulnerable groups); DPO advises and monitors; consult Commissioner if high residual risk
requirements.rationale: DPO Guideline 2025, dpo-7 — DPIA role under s129B

requirements.requirement: Facilitate Commissioner cooperation: respond to audits/inspections, respond to enquiries within specified timeframes, coordinate enforcement notice responses, maintain Commissioner interaction log
requirements.rationale: DPO Guideline 2025, dpo-8 — Commissioner cooperation under s129B

governance.summary: The DPO must have organizational independence, direct board reporting, freedom from instructions on DPO tasks, and protection from dismissal for performing duties.

requirements.requirement: Ensure DPO independence: no instructions on DPO tasks, no dismissal for performing duties, resolve conflicts of interest (CEO/CTO/CFO/HR Head cannot be DPO)
requirements.rationale: DPO Guideline 2025, dpo-5 — independence requirements under s129B
requirements.frequency: Ongoing; reviewed at appointment and annually

requirements.requirement: DPO responsibilities: advise on PDPA compliance, monitor compliance, advise on DPIAs, act as Commissioner contact, manage data subject queries, maintain ROPA, advise on breach notification
requirements.rationale: DPO Guideline 2025, dpo-4 — tasks and responsibilities under s129B

legal.summary: Section 143A, inserted by the Personal Data Protection (Amendment) Act 2024 (Act A1699), introduces mandatory data breach notification obligations. Data users must notify the Commissioner of a data breach as soon as practicable after becoming aware of it, and must notify affected data subjects where the breach is likely to result in significant harm. The precise timeframes and notification content are to be specified in subsidiary legislation.

requirements.requirement: Notify the Personal Data Protection Commissioner as soon as practicable after becoming aware of a data breach, providing all prescribed information about the breach including its nature, categories of data affected, approximate number of data subjects affected, and remediation measures taken or planned
requirements.rationale: Section 143A makes breach notification to the Commissioner mandatory; failure to notify is a criminal offence; 'as soon as practicable' requires internal escalation processes that can confirm and notify a breach within the expected regulatory timeframe

requirements.requirement: Notify affected data subjects of a data breach as soon as practicable where the breach is likely to result in significant harm to those data subjects, providing information about the nature of the breach, the personal data affected, and the steps they can take to protect themselves
requirements.rationale: Data subject notification enables affected individuals to take protective action; failure to notify where significant harm is likely denies data subjects the opportunity to mitigate harm and is an offence under s143A

requirements.requirement: Establish and document a data breach assessment procedure that enables the organisation to assess the severity and scope of a breach, determine notification obligations, and initiate the notification process within the prescribed timeframe from the point of becoming aware of the breach
requirements.rationale: The assessment procedure must be documented and tested in advance; a spontaneous response to a breach is unlikely to meet regulatory expectations for promptness

technical.summary: Technical detection, logging, and escalation capabilities must support the organisation's ability to become aware of breaches promptly and to gather the information required for regulatory and data subject notifications.

requirements.requirement: Implement security monitoring, intrusion detection, and alerting systems on all systems processing personal data that are capable of detecting potential data breaches (including unauthorised access, exfiltration, and ransomware) and generating alerts for investigation within a defined response timeframe
requirements.rationale: An organisation cannot notify of a breach it does not know about; detection capability is a prerequisite for timely notification compliance under s143A
requirements.frequency: Implemented continuously; monitoring effectiveness reviewed quarterly

requirements.requirement: Implement a breach investigation and evidence preservation process that captures forensic information about the breach (attack vector, data accessed, data subjects affected, timeline) to support regulatory notifications and any subsequent JPDP investigation
requirements.rationale: Regulatory notifications require specific factual information about the breach; forensic evidence preservation preserves the data needed for notifications and defends against regulatory allegations of under-reporting
requirements.frequency: Per breach event; procedure reviewed annually

requirements.requirement: Maintain a centralised breach log recording all actual and near-miss security incidents, including date of discovery, nature of incident, data affected, notification decisions made, and regulatory correspondence, for at least 7 years
requirements.rationale: The breach log is a key regulatory document; it demonstrates the organisation's breach history and notification compliance, and provides the record needed for regulatory inspections and internal reporting

requirements.requirement: Maintain security measures sufficient to detect breaches promptly; log all security incidents to establish breach timelines
requirements.rationale: DBN Guideline 2025, dbn-1 — breach detection under s143A

requirements.requirement: Conduct documented risk assessment for every personal data breach to determine notifiability; retain assessment for 5 years
requirements.rationale: DBN Guideline 2025, dbn-2 — notifiability assessment under s143A

requirements.requirement: Notify Commissioner within 72 hours of becoming aware of notifiable breach using prescribed JPDP form; phased notifications acceptable
requirements.rationale: DBN Guideline 2025, dbn-3 — 72-hour notification under s143A

requirements.requirement: Submit complete notification to Commissioner using prescribed form with all mandatory fields; attach supporting evidence
requirements.rationale: DBN Guideline 2025, dbn-4 — notification content under s143A

requirements.requirement: Notify affected data subjects within 7 days of determining high-risk breach; use direct communication in plain language (BM/EN); include protective recommendations
requirements.rationale: DBN Guideline 2025, dbn-5/6 — data subject notification under s143A

requirements.requirement: Maintain Data Breach Register recording all breaches (notifiable and non-notifiable); retain for 5 years; produce to Commissioner within 5 working days on request
requirements.rationale: DBN Guideline 2025, dbn-7 — record-keeping under s143A
requirements.frequency: Ongoing; updated within 24 hours of each breach

requirements.requirement: Implement immediate containment measures; conduct root cause analysis within 60 days; implement systemic remediation; submit post-breach report to Commissioner
requirements.rationale: DBN Guideline 2025, dbn-8 — post-breach remediation under s143A

governance.summary: Breach notification governance must assign clear roles and decision-making authority for breach assessment and notification, supported by a tested incident response plan and regular tabletop exercises.

requirements.requirement: Develop and maintain an Incident Response Plan (IRP) that specifically addresses the s143A notification obligations, assigns named roles for breach assessment (DPO, CISO, Legal, Communications), and specifies the internal escalation timeframe from detection to notification decision
requirements.rationale: An IRP with clear roles and timeframes ensures the organisation can meet its notification obligations without confusion about who is responsible for each step
requirements.frequency: Established; reviewed annually and after each incident

requirements.requirement: Conduct at least one annual tabletop exercise simulating a significant data breach scenario to test the IRP, the notification process, the Commissioner communication, and the data subject communication templates, and remediate any gaps identified
requirements.rationale: Annual testing of the IRP ensures that roles, processes, and templates are fit for purpose before a real incident occurs; untested plans frequently fail at critical moments
